essential_question: Why does pulling out the largest perfect square always give the fully simplified form — and why can only LIKE radicals ever combine?
objective: Justify why factoring out the largest perfect-square factor produces the fully simplified radical, justify why only like radicals can be combined, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint.

content.prompt: Without looking anything up, simplify √72 and √48 from scratch. Then check your work against your concept note and your tile log from Session 2.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 17A & 17B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 17A/17B. We check the tricky ones: the unlike-radical trap (5√2+3√5 can't combine), problems that need TWO simplifying steps, dividing radicals (numbers over numbers, radicals over radicals), and the fill-in-the-blank vocabulary (radical sign, perfect square). Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: √72 simplifies to 6√2, and no larger perfect square could have been pulled out. Argue it: list every perfect square that divides 72 evenly (1, 4, 9, 36) and show why 36 is the largest — and why nothing bigger is hiding in the leftover 2.
Claim 2: 5√2 and 3√5 can NEVER be combined into one term, no matter how you simplify them. Argue it from the definition of like radicals.
Claim 3: (3√2)(5√8) comes out to 60 with no radical left at all. Argue why multiplying two radicals can sometimes make the radical disappear completely.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 18 — The Pythagorean Theorem — where these very radicals become the EXACT lengths of a right triangle's sides.

success_criteria.text: I can simplify any radical to its simplest form and justify why no larger perfect square remains
I can justify why only like radicals combine, and correctly combine or refuse to combine given pairs
I can multiply two radicals and simplify the result, recognizing when the radical disappears entirely

questions.explain: Like radicals must share the exact same radicand — both terms here have 7 under the root, so their coefficients (4 and 10) can combine.
All three terms share the radicand 3, so just combine the coefficients: 9 + 2 − 4 = 7, giving 7√3.

questions.prompt: Simplify √8 first, then decide: can √8 and 5√2 be combined? If so, combine them.
questions.answer: √8 = √(4 × 2) = 2√2; since 2√2 and 5√2 are now like radicals, 2√2 + 5√2 = 7√2.
questions.explain: √8 looks unlike 5√2 until you simplify it — 4 is the largest perfect square dividing 8, revealing the shared radicand 2.
Numbers times numbers: 2 × 2 = 4. Radicals times radicals: √5 × √5 = 5 (the identity √a×√a=a). 4 × 5 = 20 — the radical cancels entirely since 5 × 5 = 25, a perfect square.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why 6√7 and 3√5 can NEVER be combined into one term, no matter how you simplify them.
questions.answer: Their radicands (7 and 5) are already in simplest form and are different numbers — like radicals must share the exact same number under the root, and no amount of factoring can turn 7 into 5, so the two terms stay separate forever.
questions.explain: Full credit connects the impossibility to the radicands being different prime numbers with no perfect-square factor that could reconcile them.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 17 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
